title: Poisonous Wine: 10 Mice, 1000 Bottles

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: 1000 bottles, exactly one is poisoned. Poison kills in 18 hours, no symptoms before death. You have 10 mice and 20 hours. Identify the poisoned bottle.
rules: 10 mice -> 2^10 = 1024 distinct dead/alive patterns -- enough for 1000 bottles.
Label each bottle with its 10-bit binary code.
Mouse i drinks from every bottle whose i-th bit is 1.
After 18 hours, read off the dead/alive pattern as a 10-bit number -- that's the poisoned bottle's index.
setup.question: Why does this work, and why is exactly 10 mice the minimum?

learningObjectives: Apply binary encoding to a parallel testing problem.
Reason about extensions: what if you had 17 hours instead of 20? Or 2000 bottles?

stages.label: Why 10 mice?
stages.prompt: Each mouse provides one binary outcome (dead or alive). With k mice you get 2^k distinct outcome patterns. Minimum k to distinguish 1000 bottles?
stages.explanation: Information-theoretic lower bound: distinguishing N outcomes requires log_2 N bits. With binary outcomes (dead/alive), we need at least ceil(log_2 N) tests. For N = 1000, that's 10.

stages.label: Decoding the result
stages.prompt: Mice 6, 7, and 9 die (numbering from 1). What's the poisoned bottle number?

options.text: Binary pattern with bits 6, 7, 9 set: 0101100000_2 = 2^8 + 2^6 + 2^5 = 256 + 64 + 32 = 352.  (Reading bit positions as 10..1.)

stages.explanation: Mouse i corresponds to bit i. Dead mice indicate which bits are 1 in the bottle's binary label. The bit positions are conventionally numbered from 1 = LSB to 10 = MSB; either convention works as long as you're consistent. Using the source convention (bit 10 = MSB), bottle = 0101100000_2 = 352.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: With k mice, distinguish at most __[a]__ outcomes.  For N = 1000 bottles, k = __[b]__ minimum.  For N = 2000 bottles, k = __[c]__.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What if you only had 17 hours (less than the 18 hour kill time)? (You couldn't test -- need to wait full 18.)

What if you have multiple test rounds (e.g., 38 hours)? (You can do TWO 18-hour rounds; with 5 mice and 2 rounds, encode 3^5 = 243 outcomes since each mouse has 3 states: died-round-1, died-round-2, alive.)
How many mice if 2 bottles are poisoned out of 1000? (The dead set is the OR of two binary labels -- harder to decode; need a non-adaptive group-testing design.)
